did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning (specifically Support Vector Machine, SVM) for skin cancer classification. The methodology involved preprocessing dermatoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset, segmenting lesions using the watershed algorithm, extracting texture features (Haralick features via GLCM), and classifying the lesions as normal or abnormal using SVM, achieving 94.33% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving image preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, and classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset and SVM algorithm. It reports specific results (94.33% accuracy) and is published in the proceedings of the MOL2NET'22 Conference, which classifies it as a research paper (conference paper) rather than a review.

title: Computer Vision Based Skin Cancer Classification by Using Texture Features
journal: MOL2NET'22, Conference on Molecular, Biomedical & Computational Sciences and Engineering
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of skin cancer using computer vision and machine learning techniques on dermatoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. The methodology involved preprocessing images with contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization and median filtering, followed by lesion segmentation using the watershed algorithm. Texture features were extracted from the segmented regions using the Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) to compute Haralick features such as contrast, correlation, energy, and homogeneity. The extracted features were then classified into normal and abnormal categories using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm. The model was evaluated using 10-fold cross-validation, achieving an overall accuracy of 94.33% with a Kappa statistic of 0.8867. The results demonstrate that combining optimized segmentation, texture feature extraction, and SVM classification is effective for skin lesion diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as 'Normal' in the SVM classifier output (Table 2) and 'benign' in the dataset description (300 benign skin lesions). The dataset used was HAM10000, which contains 7 specific skin disorders, but this study aggregated them into binary classes.

Classified as 'Abnormal' in the SVM classifier output (Table 2) and 'malignant' in the dataset description (300 malignant skin lesions). The dataset used was HAM10000, which contains 7 specific skin disorders, but this study aggregated them into binary classes.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'Normal' and 'Abnormal' categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states that skin lesions were classified using an SVM algorithm. The dataset (HAM10000) was aggregated into two classes: 'Normal' (benign) and 'Abnormal' (malignant). The model achieved 94.33% accuracy in distinguishing between these discrete diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (ISIC 2020 training set)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermatoscopic images containing seven skin disorders, used in this investigation. The study utilized a subset of this dataset consisting of 600 RGB skin lesion photos (150x150 pixels).

use.training: Used for training the SVM classifier via 10-fold cross-validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier on the HAM10000 dataset.
To measure the agreement between the predicted classifications and the actual classes, correcting for chance.
To detail the counts of true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives for each class (Normal and Abnormal).

statistical_methods_used.method: Error metrics (Mean absolute error, Root mean squared error, Relative absolute error, Root relative squared error)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify the deviation of the classifier's predictions from the actual values.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, Kappa statistic, and confusion matrix metrics via 10-fold cross-validation.

HAM10000 (ISIC 2020 training set).notes: The study aggregated the HAM10000 dataset into two classes: Normal (benign) and Abnormal (malignant), with 300 instances each. The SVM classifier achieved an overall accuracy of 94.33%. The paper notes that the DVM (likely typo for SVM) approach had greater accuracy, precision, and f1-score compared to other potential classifiers, though specific comparative values for other classifiers are not provided in the text.

architecture_used.input_size: 150x150 pixels (RGB images converted to grayscale for feature extraction)

architecture_details.feature_extractor: Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) for Haralick texture features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity)
architecture_details.preprocessing: Contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization, morphological occlusion, median filter

conclusions: The study successfully used machine learning, specifically Support Vector Machine (SVM), to classify skin lesions in HAM10000 dermatoscopic images after segmentation and texture feature extraction.

The SVM classifier achieved an accuracy of 94.33%, with a Kappa statistic of 0.8867, demonstrating that combining an optimized classifier, current segmentation techniques, and well-tested feature extraction strategies yields effective performance for skin lesion diagnosis.
The authors acknowledge limitations regarding the dataset size and class imbalance, suggesting that increasing training samples and balancing labeled lesion groups could improve findings.
Future improvements may involve using sophisticated classification models or deep learning techniques and training models on the complete dataset.
important_remarks: The study aggregates the HAM10000 dataset into a binary classification task (Normal/Benign vs. Abnormal/Malignant), losing the granular diagnostic information of the seven specific skin disorders originally present in the dataset.

The dataset used is a highly imbalanced subset of the original HAM10000, specifically limited to 600 images (300 benign, 300 malignant), which limits the generalizability of the findings compared to the full dataset.
The paper acknowledges class imbalance as a limitation and suggests that balancing labeled lesion groups could improve results, indicating potential bias in the current model's performance.

The model was trained and evaluated on a single dataset (HAM10000) using 10-fold cross-validation; there is no external validation on independent datasets, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific subset.
The paper contains a typographical error referring to the classifier as 'DVM' in the conclusions, which may cause confusion regarding the actual model used (SVM).
The study relies on traditional machine learning (SVM with hand-crafted texture features) rather than deep learning, which the authors themselves note as a limitation for future improvement.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance is described solely through accuracy, Kappa statistic, and confusion matrix metrics.

The preprocessing pipeline includes morphological occlusion and watershed segmentation, which may introduce segmentation errors that propagate into feature extraction and classification, though specific error rates for segmentation are not provided.
